coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as Reactive Nitrogen Species nanosensors
coredata.dc:creator: Cardoso, Marita A.
Duarte, Abel J.
Gonçalves, Helena M.R.
coredata.dc:description: Three sets of Carbon Dots (Cdots) were produced through the carbohydrates acid thermal decomposition method. These nanoparticles were functionalized with a polymer, known for its biological compatibility: polyethylene glycol, PEG200, and folic acid, FA, a biomolecule associated with the reactive oxygen and nitrogen (ROS/RNS) savaging process, thus resulting CdotsPEG200, CdotsPEG200FA and CdotsFA. These nanoparticles were tested as nitric oxide radical (NO·) sensors and it was determined that CdotsPEG200FA and CdotsFA fluorescence intensity was quenched by the presence of this radical specie. Moreover, according to the Benesi-Hilderbrand plot, the nanoparticles have a high affinity towards the analyte and this interaction is consistent with a 1:1 stoichiometry, through an independent mechanism. The Stern-Volmer constant, obtained for both sensing systems, is compatible with the formation of stable complexes (static quenching) between the Folic Acid residues on the Cdots surface and NO·. The detection and quantification limits along with the sensitivity were calculated for both nanoparticles: DL (31.7 ± 0.02) x 10−9, QL (96.29 ± 0.01) x 10−9, Sensitivity (5.2 ± 0.5) x 109 M for CdotsFA and DL (83 ± 3) x 10−10, QL (251 ± 2) x 10−10, Sensitivity (8.4 ± 0.3) x 1010 M. These values are adequate for biological sensing and are quite competitive with other reported nanosensors for NO· detection and quantification.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cdots Synthesis: Schematic representation of the Cdots synthesis.

Transmission Electron Microscopy analysis: TEM images of the produced Cdots a) raw Cdots; b) CdotsPEG200; c) CdotsPEG200FA and d) CdotsFA. These images were collected upon passing the Cdots solution through a RC 200 nm filter and 5 min ultrasonic treatment prior to drop casting the Cdots solution of the TEM grid.

a) Fluorescence Properties of the produced Cdots: Fluorescence emission profile of the raw Cdots (excitation of 330 nm), CdotsPEG200 (excitation of 350 nm), CdotsPEG200FA (excitation of 350 nm) and CdotsFA (excitation of 400 nm) b) Emission peak decomposition of CdotsPEG200FA using a Gaussian fit c) Emission decomposition peak of CdotsFA using a Gaussian fit.

Cdots sensing profile in the presence of NO: Fluorescence quenching profile due to NO· radical interaction, using a concentration range of 10−10-10−5 M and excitation wavelength of 400 nm and 350 nm for a) CdotsFA and b) CdotsPEG200FA, respectively.

Interaction profiles between the Cdots and NO: Benesi-Hildebrand plot for a) CdotsFA and b) CdotsPEG200FA with increasing amounts of NO·, using a concentration range of 10−10-10−5 M and excitation wavelength of 400 nm and 350 nm, respectively. Hill's plot of c) CdotsFA and d) CdotsPEG200FA as a function NO· in the same concentration range. These values represent an average of at least three independent measurements.

Linearization of the quenching effect induced by NO: Stern-Volmer plots for CdotsFA in blue and CdotsPEG200FA in green using the fluorescence intensity at their maximum emission wavelength and a NO· concentration range of 1.00 × 10−10-1.00 × 10−5 M. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Analytical parameters of the developed NO· nanosensors: Detection and quantification limits for a) CdotsFA and b) CdotsPEG200FA for the detection of NO· in a concentration range of 10−10-10−5 M.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as Reactive Nitrogen Species nanosensors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Three sets of Carbon Dots (Cdots) were produced through the carbohydrates acid thermal decomposition method. These nanoparticles were functionalized with a polymer, known for its biological compatibility: polyethylene glycol, PEG200, and folic acid, FA, a biomolecule associated with the reactive oxygen and nitrogen (ROS/RNS) savaging process, thus resulting CdotsPEG200, CdotsPEG200FA and CdotsFA. These nanoparticles were tested as nitric oxide radical (NO·) sensors and it was determined that CdotsPEG200FA and CdotsFA fluorescence intensity was quenched by the presence of this radical specie. Moreover, according to the Benesi-Hilderbrand plot, the nanoparticles have a high affinity towards the analyte and this interaction is consistent with a 1:1 stoichiometry, through an independent mechanism. The Stern-Volmer constant, obtained for both sensing systems, is compatible with the formation of stable complexes (static quenching) between the Folic Acid residues on the Cdots surface and NO·. The detection and quantification limits along with the sensitivity were calculated for both nanoparticles: DL (31.7 ± 0.02) x 10−9, QL (96.29 ± 0.01) x 10−9, Sensitivity (5.2 ± 0.5) x 109 M for CdotsFA and DL (83 ± 3) x 10−10, QL (251 ± 2) x 10−10, Sensitivity (8.4 ± 0.3) x 1010 M. These values are adequate for biological sensing and are quite competitive with other reported nanosensors for NO· detection and quantification.

ce:section.ce:para: It is a known fact that high reactivity is associated with difficulties in biological media. The most know examples are Reactive Nitrogen Species (RNS) and Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS). In this sense the study of these species in vivo is an expanding area [1,2]. Nitric oxide (NO·) is a RNS radical that, in a similar way of its radical counterparts, ROS, can be biological synthesized by a family of different nitrogen monoxide synthases from the amino acid l-arginine [3]. These highly reactive species are prone to toxicological effects on living organisms and, as such, several studies have been performed in order to better understand their biodistribution, production and toxicity [3,5]. Even though the toxicological issues of ROS and RNS are known for some time, more recently it has been suggested that they also play an important biological role as secondary messengers. Indeed, not only NO· but also its oxidation by-products (nitrogen dioxide (NO·2), dinitrogen trioxide (N2O3), peroxynitrite (ONOO−), nitrite (NO2 −), nitrate (NO3 −), and nitroxyl (HNO)) have been suggested has participants in several physiological events, namely, pathologies such as ischemia, inflammation, cancer, and neurodegeneration [6], neurotransmission [7], host immune system defense [8], among others [9–11].

Due to their biological role the interest in developing strategies for highly sensitive and specific RNS sensors has been growing. One of the first sensors for RNS was based on fluoresceins [12,13]. This organic fluorophore has numerous applications due to its high fluorescence intensity. However, this optical property is also quite sensitive to pH changes and, as most organic fluorophores, is prone to photobleaching and photoinstability [13]. In order to overcome the drawbacks of fluorescein, new sensors based on Rhodamine [14] and cyanine [15] were developed. From the analysis of the available organic sensors for RNS it is possible to see a trend between the sensing ability and the presence of vicinal amines. This characteristic has been pointed out as fundamental for RNS sensing [14]. Some examples of vicinal amines sensors for RNS are: o-phenylenediamine and its derivatives [15] and vicinal diaminobenzoacridine molecules [16]. Indeed, the most common RNS sensors are organic-based. Most organic fluorophores present low photostability, irreversibility and indirect measurements that are clear disadvantages for in vivo sensing. Nonetheless, they have been greatly used for ROS sensing and some outstanding results have been obtained [17].

In a more recent approach some new strategies were developed and the obtained results are quite promising, namely the use of nanotubes [18], cellular-based [19,20], and protein-based designs [21]. Despite all these new developments the major challenges are: the need for a low detection limit in biological media and, the necessity to reduce the background noise inherent to imaging studies. In an attempt to overcome these challenges the most recent strategy in developing NO· sensors is based in its retention inside the cell. In these sensing systems, the fluorophore penetrates the cell and them accumulates inside it, this way there will be more intracellular sensor available for reaction, as well as, lower background noise [22].

Overall it is possible to say that the effect of NO· on the body is largely dependent of the local environment. Most of the NO· produced is oxidized to form nitrite ion or nitrate, as such, it is not surprising that one of the in vivo sensing mechanisms for accessing the NO· concentration is through the measurement of free nitrite and nitrate levels [23]. Indeed, despite the fact that the effects of NO· on biological media are known, the mechanism by which they occur is still somewhat blur [17]. In this sense, there is a clear need for the development of specific sensors for ROS and RNS in the nanomolar range and also that these sensors enable the in situ, in vivo, tracking of these molecules.

Carbon Dots (Cdots) are the newest member of the Quantum Dot (QDs) family. Ever since their discovery the number of applications using these carbon-based nanoparticles has grown [24–28]. One of the Cdots most interesting features is the ability to be functionalized with different molecules according to their intended application. Recently it has been reported the chemotherapeutic activity of Cdots when associated with known anticancer agents, such as, doxorubicin [29–31]. Moreover, they have also been used as NO· delivery agents when associated with known molecular NO· donors, such as S-nitrosothiol [32], 4-nitro-2-(trifluoromethyl)aniline [33] and N-diazeniumdiolate [29]. The association between these molecules and Cdots fluorescence properties, namely the possibility of using two-photon excitation [30,32] to monitor the NO· release using biologically safe wavelengths has been a major breakthrough in the field. The main issues on these systems are: the need to use toxic organic solvents in the Cdots synthesis along with the time consuming synthetic and purification procedures. Moreover, according to the adopted procedure it may also be necessary to employ high pressure [29] or/and temperature [29,33] and controlled atmosphere [33] to obtain water soluble Cdots.

Here it is reported a facile, straightforward synthesis of Cdots through glucose acid thermal decomposition and in situ functionalization with Poly(ethylene glycol)200 (PEG200) and folic acid (FA) that are effective NO· nanosensors. The synthesis was performed at low temperatures, without the need of controlled atmosphere using green solvents. In order to obtain effective nanosensors for NO·, a careful thought on the functionalizing molecules was taken; indeed, these molecules were not chosen randomly. It has been previously reported that the presence of PEG200 on the surface of QDs has significantly inhibit the cytotoxicity and immune responses of keratinocytes when compared to QDs without this capping [34]. These results suggest that PEG coating is an effective approach for a safe use of nanoparticles for in vivo applications [35,36]. On the other hand, FA is a biomolecule which inhibits possible toxicity responses. The choice of FA was also due to the possibility of this molecule to interact with Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) or/and Reactive Nitrogen Species (RNS). In fact it has been proven that the use of FA allowed the detection of peroxynitrite [37]. At the end an evaluation of this sensor and its possible applicability in biological media will be addressed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cdots Synthesis and in situ functionalization
ce:section.ce:para: All chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used without further purification. The nanoparticles were produced using an adapted procedure of the known carbohydrates thermal acid decomposition method [38,39]. Briefly, the Cdots were produced by dissolving a 10 g of glucose in 100 mL of deionized water to form a clear solution. Afterwards concentrated 2 mL of HNO3 was added and the mixture was ultrasonically treated for 1 h and oven-dried (60 °C) for 1 h. Subsequently 20 mL of PEG200 were added to the resulting solution and left in reflux for 1 h. This led to the production of Cdots functionalized with PEG200. To produce the Cdots functionalized with PEG200 and Folic acid (FA), 20 mL of the CdotsPEG200 were added to 30 mL of folic acid (1.25 μM) and left in reflux for 1 h. The synthesis of Cdots functionalized only with FA (CdotsFA), was performed using a similar procedure of CdotsPEG200FA, the only difference was that the precursor was the raw Cdots solution. The Cdots solution passed through an initial absence of color; to a light brown upon functionalization with PEG200 (CdotsPEG200); to a light yellow after the addition of folic acid (CdotsPEG200FA). Upon the Cdots production a purification step was undertaken were the solutions were inserted in dialysis molecular bags (cutoff molecular weight: 100–500 D) against deionized water for 24 h. The purified Cdots solutions were diluted to obtain the sensing solutions: 1 mL of the purified Cdots solution was diluted into 100 mL of water).

The sensitivity of the functionalized Cdots (CdotsPEG200 and Cdots PEG200FA) towards ROS and RNS, as well as, standard solutions of NO·, H2O2, Fe(II), ONOO-, KO2, KNO2 were tested. In order to do so, a titration procedure was followed for addition in situ of the testing analyte. All titrations were performed by adding small aliquots of the standard solutions to 800 μL of Cdots (the dilutions where tested so all Cdots solutions had the same initial fluorescence intensity). This was performed in such a way that the concentration of all species ranged between 1.00 × 10−10-1.90 × 10−5 M.

The fluorescence emission and excitation profiles were recorded using the Varian Cary Eclipse fluorimeter. The parameters were: (i) For raw Cdots: excitation of 330 nm and emission range of 330–650 nm; (ii) For CdotsPEG200: excitation of 350 nm and emission range of 350–650 nm; (iii) For CdotsPEG200FA: excitation of 400 nm and emission range of 400–650 nm; (iv) For CdotsFA: excitation of 400 nm and emission range of 400–650 nm. Regardless of the sample an integration time of 0.1 s and slit of 5 nm were used. In all these experiments quartz cells were used.

The size distribution of Cdots in water was determined by Dynamic Light Scattering analysis using a Malvern Instruments (Malvern, UK) Zeta Sizer Nano ZS, using disposable polystyrene cells from Sigma.
Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) of the raw and functionalized Cdots was performed on a Jeol JEM 1400, Zeiss model EM 10C with a SC1000 Orius TM CCD camera gatan using TEM copper-based grids.

The X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a Kratos AXIS Ultra HSA, with VISION software for data acquisition and CasaXPS software for data analysis. A monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.7 eV), operating at 15 kV (90 W), in fixed analyser transmission (FAT) mode, with a pass energy of 40 eV for regions ROI and 80 eV was used. Data acquisition was performed with a pressure lower than 1.0 × 10−6 Pa and a charge neutralization system. The electric charge effect was corrected using the carbon peak (285 eV) as reference. The XPS deconvolution spectra was performed using the XPSPEAK41 or CasaXPS programs, with Gaussian-Lorentzian peak fitting and Shirley type background subtraction.

The data resulting from the titrations were consistent to a quenching phenomenon induced by the presence of the analyte. As such, the data were analyzed using the simplified Stern-Volmer equation:

The Cdots were produced and functionalized in situ using a two-step approach (Fig. 1 ): the decomposition of glucose to form the nanoparticles (raw Cdots) and their further functionalization with folic acid (CdotsFA), PEG200 (CdotsPEG200) and both molecules (CdotsPEG200FA).

The produced Cdots were analyzed using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). As it is possible to see from Fig. 2 , there is a trend between the functionalization and the size of the nanoparticle. Before performing these TEM analyses, all samples were passed through a RC 200 nm filter, and the solution containing the Cdots was diluted to try to prevent agglomeration [25–28,43]. Nevertheless, as it is visible on Fig. 2a, the Cdots tend to agglomerate into larger nanoparticles. In fact, since they are highly reactive, due to the reactive surface groups and dangling bonds, it is easy to understand this tendency [25–28]. Furthermore, when the nanoparticles are functionalized with PEG200 the polymer seems to form a film that “entraps” several particles inside, forming what it seems to be a polymer shell (Fig. 2b). It is also interesting to notice that the CdotsPEG200FA (Fig. 2c), has a material with different density than the nanoparticles surrounding them, but the shape is different from the one observed for CdotsPEG200. This difference is most probably related to the double functionalization of these nanoparticles. Since both functionalization molecules were added at the same time, some competition for the Cdots surface bounds must have happened and the overall shape of the nanoparticles was consequently affected by it [43]. On Fig. 2d it is possible to see CdotsFA. These nanoparticles are also round-shaped and the main difference between these and, CdotsPEG200 and CdotsPEG200FA, is the absence of the polymer structure. Nonetheless, it is possible to see a high density region, probably the carbon core/cores and a different density region that resembles a shell, probably formed by the FA molecules.

These Cdots were also subjected to a DLS analysis (Supplementary Fig. S1). The size trend found in TEM analysis was further supported by these DLS data. It is relevant to emphasize that the sample preparation for DLS analysis was the same of TEM, therefore any nanoparticles larger than 200 nm should correspond to Cdots agglomerates and not individual particles.

In order to better ascertain the functional groups on the Cdots surface an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed to the CdotsPEG200; CdotsPEG200FA and CdotsFA (Supplementary Fig. S2). CdotsPEG200 XPS spectra shows only two distinct peaks, that can be attributed to C1s and O1s (Supplementary Figs. S2a and b). This was expected, since the Cdots were obtained from glucose and the functionalization molecule (PEG200) was constituted by the same atomic elements. The C1s spectrum (Supplementary Fig. S2a) displays two distinct peaks at 285.0 eV (C–C sp2) and 286.6 eV (C–O bound) [44,45]. On the other hand, the introduction of folic acid residues incorporated nitrogen atoms, hence CdotsPEG200FA and CdotsFA XPS spectra exhibit three peaks, that can be attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s (Supplementary Figs. S2c–h). The CdotsPEG200FA C1s spectrum has three different peaks at 285.7 eV, 287.3 eV and 294.0 eV, that correspond to C–C sp2, C–O/C–N and CO bounds, respectively (Supplementary Fig. S2c). CdotsFA C1s only displayed two peaks that are consistent with C–C sp2 and C–O/C–N bounds (Supplementary Fig. S2f). The N1s spectra of both Cdots exhibit two distinct peaks that can be attributed to the pyrrolic and pyridinic N–C bounds (Supplementary Figs. S2d and g). Lastly the O1s spectra of both compounds also display two peaks that can be attributed to CO and C–OH, respectively (Supplementary Figs. S2e and h). Analysing the data it is possible to stablish the relationship between the functional groups on the Cdots surface and the reagents used to produce the different Cdots. Also, the presence of N in the surface groups is associated with the use of FA, thus proving the Cdots functionalization with this molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and mechanism of the produced cdots
ce:section.ce:para: Over the years several authors have tried to explain the optical properties and the fluorescence mechanism of Cdots [43,46,47]; however, no consensus has been obtained so far. The large number of variables, introduced by the numerous synthetic paths, functionalization procedures and surface chemistry/defects has added further complexity to the subject [48,49]. Currently, it is thought that the different emission mechanisms of these nanoparticles are due to three main reasons: (i) the core emission due to the conjugated π-domains of the carbon core; (ii) the surface states introduced by the different chemical composition of the functionalizing agents and; (iii) the molecular state, where it is considered that the emission originates from free or bounded fluorescent molecules [50].

The acid carbohydrates thermal decomposition synthetic pathway by which the raw Cdots were produced has been used with success and was one of the first pathways for these nanoparticles production [43]. It has the advantage of decomposing a carbon raw material into carbon nanoclusters that reassemble at an atomic state to form fluorescent Cdots [51,52]. One of the most appealing characteristics of the Cdots is the fact that if upon production the nanoparticles surface is not suitable for the intended application, it is possible to further functionalize it with other molecules of interest accordingly [25,26]. Indeed, the ability of functionalizing (doping) the Cdots with different molecules is one of its most appealing features that affects not only the optical properties/fluorescence mechanism, but also the sensing capacities. Upon the Cdots production by the acid carbohydrates thermal decomposition, irreversible carbonization takes places to form the carbon clusters that will lead to the Cdots formation [51–53]. The carbonization reactions are slightly different according to the raw material and this leads to structural differences in the resulting nanoparticles that are reflected in their optical properties.

Hence the choice of the functionalization molecules needs to be carefully thought out according to the intended application. The use of PEG200 as capping agent has been described as an effective way to inhibit cytotoxicity and immune responses [17,26]. Even though it is not expected to see cytotoxicity issues arising from the use of Cdots, since there are numerous papers that reinforce the non-toxicity nature of these nanoparticles [54], we decided to use a three way approach for the production of ROS/RNS sensors: (i) to functionalize the Cdots with this polymer and see if it is possible to use them for ROS/RNS sensing; (ii) to functionalize the raw Cdots with two different molecules and evaluate the efficacy they provide to detect ROS/RNS and; finally (iii) to functionalize the raw Cdots with only Folic acid and determine their sensing ability. Each time an unreported synthesis is performed it is necessary to take into account the size growth, size homogeneity and their relationship with fluorescence intensity. By using these parameters, it was possible to determine that the best reaction time for each experience was 1 h for the initial CdotsPEG200 and CdotsFA. For Cdots PEG200FA the synthesis started with the CdotsPEG200, therefore we need to consider the time required to produce this starting material (1 h) and to produce CdotsPEG200 (1 h). Hence, the complete synthesis took 2 h.

The production of all these nanoparticles was a good way to evaluate the effect that each functionalizing molecule had on the Cdots optical properties and, consequently, on the fluorescence mechanism. Indeed, the Cdots chemical composition and morphological structure is closely related to the optical properties they exhibit [50]. In this sense it is interesting to notice that each of the synthetized nanoparticles exhibit unique optical properties (Table 1 ). Indeed, with the exception of CdotsPEG200 and CdotsPEG200FA, all other have different maximum excitations, emissions, intensity and full width at half maximum (FWHM). Traditionally, non-functionalized Cdots are less emissive than when doped with nitrogen, sulfur, chlorine and phosphor atoms [53]. In our case, since the raw Cdots where produced from glucose; it is presumed that the surface groups are mainly composed by H, C and O. Hence, the functionalization with PEG200 did not alter the chemical composition, but it provided new electronic delocalization pathways for the Cdots nucleus π-electrons surface onto the functionalization agent. This effect is usually associated with a boost in the fluorescence intensity signal [54] and in this case it was possible to observe a drastic increase (Table 1). In fact, when comparing the raw Cdots with the CdotsPEG200, CdotsPEG200FA and CdotsFA a fluorescence intensity increase of 95%, 92% and 89% was detected at their maximum excitation wavelengths. Thereby demonstrating that, a feasible way to increase the photoluminescence properties of the Cdots passes through their functionalization. Additionally, it is also interesting to highlight that the introduction of FA allows doping the Cdots with nitrogen atoms which is usually associated with higher radiative degree states and thus to a superior fluorescence intensity. However, this was not the observed case. Indeed, the introduction of an organic polymer (PEG) on the Cdots surface had a fluorescence intensity superior to the introduction of an organic acid with an N heteroatom (Table 1). Moreover, even when comparing the signals obtained for CdotsPEG200 and CdotsPEG200FA, the traditional trend of a higher fluorescence intensity associated with N atoms on the Cdots surface was not observed. This may be due to the PEG200 ability to act as an effective ligand that creates an asymmetric electric field and boosts the fluorescence intensity of fluorophores [55]. This effect seems to surpass the boost provided by the introduction of the previously mentioned atoms. Indeed, it has been previously reported by Saito & Koketsu (2018) [55] a fluorescence intensity increase of 80-fold through the addition of PEG to Europium (III) solutions.

In the produced Cdots there is another interesting feature that seems to break the most commonly described optical properties of these nanoparticles. Usually, the introduction of N atoms (pyridinic, graphitic and amines) on the Cdots surface is not only followed by an increase in the fluorescence intensity but also blue shift in the emission wavelength [50,56]. This property has been used in the past in order to tune the emission wavelength profile of the Cdots [57]. However, the analysis of Table 1 and Fig. 3 clearly shows that the introduction of FA on raw Cdots was followed by a 15% red shift of the maximum emission wavelength. This may be due to the delocalization of excitons that is promoted by electronic coupling between nanoparticles core and FA molecules or even due to the coupling between nearby cores [58]. Moreover, it is interesting to notice that the addition of PEG200 on the Cdots surface introduced a high degree of hydroxylation that caused an 8% red shift of the emission, as expected [53], which is roughly half of the shift observed for CdotsFA. On the other hand, the simultaneous addition of PEG200 and FA on the Cdots surface has no outcome in terms of maximum emission and excitation wavelengths shifts. This is quite interesting has it indicates that the molecules attached on the surface have some effect on the quantum confinement of the nanoparticles, as previously mentioned for Cdots [59]. Furthermore, it suggests that the presence of PEG200 acts as a coating agent and prevents any further functionalization from affecting the quantum confinement in a measurable way, hence no measurable variations on the emission and excitation profiles is observed.

Another relevant feature of the Cdots is the typical narrow FWHM, usually between 70 and 90 nm [60]. This characteristic is useful when attempting to use ratiometric sensing approaches in bioimaging, but it can also be seen as a drawback for the Cdots application on white LEDs with high color rendering index [60]. The produced Cdots have an increasing trend on the FWHM according to the functionalization agent. Indeed, the introduction of molecules on the Cdots surface was followed by an increase in the FWHM (Table 1, Fig. 3), ranging from 58 nm for raw Cdots, to 88, 113 and 162 nm for CdotsPEG200, CdotsPEG200FA and CdotsFA, respectively. It is important to highlight that the values obtained for CdotsPEG200FA are among the uppermost reported in the literature [60–62] and that the FWHM of CdotsFA is the highest reported value, up to the author's knowledge. The FWHM is related to the homogeneity of the sample, in this sense it seems that the functionalization steps led to a high degree of inhomogeneity, probably due to aggregation between nearby groups [58], which is supported by the DLS analysis (Supplementary Fig. S1). The highly delocalized FA molecular structure with multiple connection sites probably led to aggregation between neighboring nanoparticles, thus leading to an unusually high FWHM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cdots performance to ROS, RNS and metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The biological concentration of ROS and RNS needs to be carefully maintained since it can have drastic consequences that culminate in cell death [1]. Therefore, the development of non-toxic sensors that can dynamically monitor the in vivo concentration of these species is quite interesting. Up to date several diamino and metal complexes have been developed for radical species sensing in biological medium [1,4]; however, molecular fluorescent sensors are prone to photobleaching and photoinstability which hinders their application. To surpass these typical drawbacks of molecular fluorescence, researchers have turned to the development of nanoprobes [1,63,64].

In this study we aimed the development an effective, specific RNS sensor system. In this sense the Cdots surface was covered with either PEG200 (CdotsPEG200), PEG200 and Folic acid (CdotsPEG200FA) and only Folic acid (CdotsFA). These functionalized Cdots were tested for radical nitrogen oxide (NO·) sensing. The charge neutrality of this radical along with its high diffusion capacity and short half-life are some of the major barriers for its detection and quantification [65]. Indeed, a simple measurement ex vivo without preventing its rapid conversion to nitrate during blood sampling is unlikely to present reproducible and accurate results for the endogenous NO· synthesis and presence [65].

It has been reported that FA has a redox regulator role in living organisms [66]. The high electronic delocalization of this biomolecule along with the presence of ketones and carboxylic acid groups made this molecule particularly interesting for RNS sensing. In Fig. 4 a, it is possible to see that the presence of 10−10 M of NO· effectively quenched the fluorescence intensity of the CdotsFA. Overall, in a concentration range of 10−5-10−10 M, the Cdots fluorescence intensity was quenched by 25%, thus allowing a good discrimination in a sub-nanomolar concentration that is compatible to the expected physiological concentrations (between 1 μM to 10 nM). Moreover, the fluorescence quenching is followed by a small maximum emission wavelength red shift of 8.3 nm. In fact, the emission wavelength profile changes from a Lorentzian to a Gaussian shape as the NO· concentration increases. This seems to indicate that the presence of this radical has an effect on the order of the system. To better understand the mechanism that lies beneath the radical interaction with the CdotsFA and the reaction stoichiometry, the Benesi-Hilderbrand equation was applied. The resulting plot (Fig. 5 a) allowed the determination of the binding constant, KB
, of 3.5 ± 2.3 (x105) M. The magnitude of this constant is an indication of a high affinity between the nanoparticles and the analyte. Moreover, the linear trend obtained for the Benesi-Hildebrand plot is consistent with a 1:1 stoichiometry [51,52]. Additional information of the interaction between CdotsFA and NO· can be obtained through the Hill's equation. This parameter provides important information on the type of binding between species. Indeed, if a molecule has more than one binding site, it is possible that the interaction of this molecule with other particles or ligands affects the interaction with the intended analyte. In the case of CdotsFA, a Hill's number,
n
, of 1.0 ± 0.1 was obtained (Fig. 5b). This is an indication that the interaction between the nanoparticles and the analyte is governed by an independent mechanism [51,52,67].

On the other hand the CdotsPEG200FA had a much dramatic response to NO·. Indeed, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 74% when exposed to the same concentration range of NO· (Fig. 4b). In order to access if this increase in sensitivity was due to PEG200, the same procedure was adopted to CdotsPEG200. It was determined that the CdotsPEG200 fluorescence intensity decrease (4%) was negligible when compared to the other functionalized Cdots. Using a similar procedure than the one adopted for CdotsFA to ascertain the mechanism of interaction between the nanoparticles and the RNS specie, allowed the determination of a binding constant, KB
, of 7.3 ± 3.6 (x104) M (Fig. 5c). Again, this order of magnitude is consistent with a high affinity, has for CdotsFA. However, and even though CdotsPEG200FA have a more dramatic reaction to the presence of NO·, the order of magnitude of the binding constant is lower; indicating that this radical has more affinity towards the CdotsFA. This may be due to the double functionalization of the CdotsPEG200FA, where less FA molecules can attach to the Cdots surface, thus decreasing the affinity towards the analyte. This fact seems to be supported by the fact that CdotsPEG200 had no measurable response (1% fluorescence quenching) to NO·.

The Benesi-Hildebrand plot is characterized by a linear trend that, as previously mentioned for CdotsFA, is an indication of a 1:1 stoichiometry between the nanoparticles and the analyte [51,52]. The Hill's plot (Fig. 5d) also provided relevant information, with the attainment of Hill's number,
n
, of 1.0 ± 0.1, e.g., the CdotsPEG200FA reaction with NO· is also governed by an independent mechanism [51,52,67]. This is quite interesting, has it indicates that despite the surface changes introduced by adding two molecules, PEG200 and FA, instead of just FA, had no difference in the type of mechanism by which the nanoparticles interact with this radical specie. Moreover, since the decrease in the fluorescence intensity is not due to the presence of PEG200, the obtained results can be due to two factors: (i) the presence of PEG200 completely covers the surface of the Cdots, as it was possible to see in Fig. 2b, therefore the surface it not available to interact with RNS; (ii) the presence of PEG200 and FA leads to bigger nanoparticles, therefore there is more FA molecules on the dots surface, which may be responsible for the increase in sensitivity.

Since in both nanoparticles the interaction between them and the analyte results in fluorescence quenching, it is possible to apply the Stern-Volmer equation and determine the Stern-Volmer constant, K
sv
(Fig. 6 ). According to the obtained data, the K
sv
for the synthetized Cdots was 2.3 ± 0.2 (x103) M and 2.28 ± 0.07 (x104) M for CdotsFA and CdotsPEG200FA, respectively. The K
sv
order of magnitude for both nanoparticles is compatible with the formation of stable complexes (static quenching) between the Folic acid residues on the Cdots surface and NO· [40].

There are only few reliable methods for detection of free radicals in biological systems but all are limited by specificity and sensitivity [68]. Another drawback is the limited half-life of this radical (≈5 s) [69]. Indeed, the high reactivity rate of NO· combined with its biological synthesis forms a complex problem that it is not easy to surpass [70]. Hence the pressing need for a specific, highly sensitive sensor, with low detection and quantification limits ( DL and QL , respectively). Moreover, due to the low half-life it would be a major advantage to develop a sensing system that can be applied for in vivo imaging, thus avoiding ex vivo extrapolation analysis.

The use of Cdots functionalized only with FA and with PEG200 and FA have a linear range between 10−10-10−5 M (Fig. 7 ). Moreover, the detection and quantification limits of these two sensing systems were determined through linear regression (Fig. 7). It is interesting to notice that CdotsFA have DL and sensitivity values inferior to CdotsPEG200FA. Nonetheless, both nanoparticles led to quite interesting and appealing DL and QL since they fall right into the biological window for RNS measurements. Moreover, they are competitive with other reported nanosensors for NO· detection and quantification. Indeed, over the years several nanosensors have been developed for NO· determination in aqueous solutions. Eliana et al. [71], for example, reported a linear concentration range from 5 to 200 μM with a DL of 1.86 μM using CdSe Quantum Dots stabilized by CS and mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA). Also using the traditional Cadmium-based Quantum Dots functionalized with trithanolamine, Jin et al. [72], obtain a DL of 302 nM. Another interesting approach was reported by Sun et al. where CdSe/ZnSTOPO based hydrophobic QDs embedded with ferric dithiocarbamate (DTC) complex (QDs-Fe (III) (DTC)3) and a DL of 3.3 μM. Carbon Dots have also been used for RNS sensing. Indeed, Eliana et al. [73] have reported a sulfur and nitrogen capped Cdots with a linear response range for NO· of 1–25 mM and a DL of 0.3 mM, which is considerably lower than the one previously obtained by this author using traditional Quantum dots [71]. Y. Wu et al. [74], also produced fluorescent Cdots that can detect this radical specie using a FRET process between naphthalimide and the Cdots. This indirect method for NO· sensing has a quite interesting DL of 3 nM; however, it relies on indirect measurements that are more prone to interferences.

In order to better access the sensing ability of these Cdots, possible interferents were tested (Supplementary Fig. S3) and it was determined that in the presence of peroxynitrite, hydrogen peroxide, superoxide and nitrite, the maximum quenching observed was 4% and 6%, for CdotsFA and CdotsPEG200FA, respectively, in the presence of nitrite. Considering the fluctuations observed in fluorescence intensity measurements, these values are almost negligible.

Therefore, both synthetized nanoparticles present themselves as very good candidates for NO· sensing. Since Cdots have been used in the past as bioimaging agents [75], it is our opinion that the here reported Cdots can provide a useful tool for in vivo imaging of this radical specie and ultimately allow a better knowledge on its biological concentration and overall mechanism.

Cdots produced from carbohydrates acid thermal decomposition were functionalized with two molecules: PEG200 and FA, thus obtaining CdotsPEG200 and CdotsFA, respectively. In order to better explore the fluorescence mechanism of these nanoparticles, a third Cdots batch was prepared, where Cdots functionalized with PEG200 where further doped with FA, CdotsPEG200FA. According to the obtained data, CdotsPEG200 had the highest fluorescence intensity when compared to the other nanoparticles. This is quite relevant, since the functionalization with FA led to the introduction of N atoms on the nanoparticles surface, and this fact is usually followed by an increase in the fluorescence intensity. These results further support the theory that in Cdots the fluorescence properties and mechanism are affected by the synergetic effect between the π-domains and the surface/edge states.

These nanoparticles were tested for NO· sensing using a low concentration range: 10−10-10−5 M in order to test for their possible application in biosensing systems. It was determined that the fluorescence intensity of CdotsPEG200FA and CdotsFA were effectively quenched by the presence of this radical specie. Moreover, since CdotsPEG200 were not sensitive to in the NO· presence, it is possible to say that the sensing effect of these Cdots is due to the presence of FA residues. To better understand the interaction mechanism between the Cdots and the analyte, the Benesi-Hilderbrand equation was applied and binding constants, KB
, of 3.5 ± 2.3 (x105) and 7.3 ± 3.6 (x104) M, were obtained for CdotsFA and CdotsPEG200FA, respectively. The magnitude of these constants is consistent with a high affinity between the Cdots and the analyte. Moreover the linear trend found in these plots is an indication of a 1:1 stoichiometry. Additionally a Hill's number,
n
, of 1.0 ± 0.1, was determined for both nanoparticles which further support the fact that this interaction is governed by an independent mechanism, where the attachment of one NO· molecules does not limits or interferes with the interaction of Cdots with another NO· molecule. The Stern-Volmer constant, K
sv
, was also calculated in 2.3 ± 0.2 (x103) and 2.28 ± 0.07 (x104) M, for CdotsFA and CdotsPEG200FA, respectively. The order of magnitude of these constants is compatible with the formation of stable complexes (static quenching) between the FA residues on Cdots surface and NO·. To further analyze the possible application of these nanoparticles as effective nanosensors, the detection and quantification limits along with the system sensitivity were calculated through linear regression. It was determined that both nanoparticles present sub-nanomolar limits and high sensitivity. Moreover, they are competitive with other reported nanosensors for NO· detection and quantification. Additionally, the presence of other possible interferents, namely peroxynitrite, hydrogen peroxide, superoxide and nitrite has a negligible quenching effect for all the interferent species and very low for nitrite (4% and 6% for CdotsFA and CdotsPEG200FA, respectively). Therefore, the developed Cdots pose as a highly attractive alternative to the current RNS sensors.
